title: Shop-Made Corner Clamp
summary: A slotted plywood corner clamp that flexes to squeeze a mitered frame joint closed instead of just pinning the corner.
description:
Most improvised corner clamps just pin a joint at 90° without applying any closing pressure across the miter itself — this one is built specifically to squeeze the two mitered faces together, which is what actually closes a gap in a loose-fitting miter.

Slots cut into the plywood base let the jaw sections **flex inward** when you tighten the wing nuts, drawing the two sides of the joint together rather than just holding them in place. That flex is the entire mechanism — there's no moving hardware beyond the bolts themselves.

**Orient the counterbored bolt slot on the jaw blocks facing down, against the base** — mounting it facing up is an easy mistake that a previous builder ran into, and it keeps the bolt heads from interfering with the clamping action.
timeLabel: 45 min–1.25 hrs plus glue drying time

tools.note: Holds the jaw blocks in place while their glue cures.
Cuts the flex slots in the base.

cutList.note: Slotted to let the jaw sections flex inward.
Angled face; counterbored bolt slot faces DOWN against the base.

steps.title: Cut the slotted base
steps.body: Cut the plywood base with slots that let the fixed jaw sections flex inward under pressure.

steps.title: Glue on the jaw blocks
steps.body: Glue the angled hardwood jaw blocks to the base, making sure the counterbored bolt slot on each block faces down against the base, not up.

steps.title: Drill for the bolts
steps.body: Drill through the base and jaw blocks for the carriage bolts.

steps.title: Assemble
steps.body: Assemble the clamp with the carriage bolts, washers, and wing nuts.

steps.title: Use it
steps.body: Clamp a mitered corner by tightening the wing nuts, which draws the flexing jaw sections together across the miter.
